clues: The computer has been getting progressively slower and programs sometimes freeze for several seconds.
User mentions hearing an unusual clicking or grinding noise from the tower.
Several files in the user's documents folder appear corrupted or missing.
On the last reboot, the PC got stuck on 'Preparing Automatic Repair.'
explanation: The clicking/grinding noise is a classic sign of mechanical HDD head failure, and file corruption plus repair loops indicate deteriorating sectors.
fixSteps: Back up data immediately if any access is still possible
Run chkdsk or manufacturer diagnostic tool to confirm
Replace the drive and restore from backup or image

clues: Applications crash randomly and the computer occasionally shows blue screens with memory-related errors.
The system sometimes fails to POST, emitting a continuous beep code.
Large file transfers occasionally fail with cyclic redundancy checks (CRC) errors.
Blue Screen of Death (BSOD) with stop code 'MEMORY_MANAGEMENT'.
MemTest86 reports thousands of errors within the first pass.
explanation: Random application crashes, 'MEMORY_MANAGEMENT' BSODs, and MemTest86 errors are textbook symptoms of defective Random Access Memory.
fixSteps: Isolate the faulty stick by running diagnostics on one module at a time
Ensure the RAM is properly seated in the motherboard slots
Replace the defective memory module with a compatible stick

clues: The web browser has new toolbars and features that the user didn't install, and performance has degraded.
CPU and network usage are consistently high, even when idle.
Pop-up advertisements appear on the desktop, not just in the browser.
The default search engine keeps changing back to an unfamiliar site.
Antivirus software has been inexplicably disabled and won't turn back on.
explanation: High idle resource usage, unauthorized browser changes, disabled antivirus, and system-wide pop-ups strongly suggest a malicious software infection.
fixSteps: Disconnect the infected machine from the network to prevent spreading
Boot into Safe Mode and run a full offline malware scan
If the infection persists, re-image or reinstall the OS from a clean state

clues: User cannot reach a website they need for work.
Other websites like Google and internal IP addresses load perfectly fine.
Using the 'ping' command returns 'could not find host'.
Other users on the same subnet are not experiencing this issue.
Running 'ipconfig /all' shows a manual, external server instead of the internal one.
explanation: Being able to reach IPs but not hostnames, combined with an incorrect primary server configured on the client, points squarely to a DNS routing issue.
fixSteps: Check the adapter settings to ensure DNS is set to automatic (DHCP) or the correct internal server
Run 'ipconfig /flushdns' to clear the local resolver cache
Verify the DHCP scope isn't handing out incorrect DNS settings

clues: A laptop becomes extremely slow and uncomfortable to touch after being used for a while.
The system fan is running at maximum speed constantly.
Performance throttles heavily during video calls or opening heavy apps.
The device spontaneously shuts down after 15-20 minutes of use.
HWMonitor shows CPU temperatures exceeding 95°C.
explanation: Extremely high recorded temperatures, aggressive thermal throttling, and sudden protective shutdowns are the direct results of an overheating system.
fixSteps: Use compressed air to clean out dust from the intake and exhaust vents
Ensure the laptop is used on a hard, flat surface rather than soft materials
If out of warranty, open the chassis to reapply thermal paste and check the fan bearings

clues: Printing a complex document causes problems while simple text pages print without issue.
Attempting to print a specific PDF causes the application to crash.
The printer outputs pages with garbled wingdings characters randomly.
The issue persists even after a reboot and clearing the print spooler.
The installed device software is a generic 'Class Driver' instead of the manufacturer's.
explanation: Garbled output and crashing on complex documents (like PDFs), despite basic printing working, usually means the print driver cannot properly render the page description language (PCL/PostScript).
fixSteps: Remove the existing printer queue and delete the driver from Print Management
Download the latest specific driver from the manufacturer's official support site
Install the new driver and print a test page to verify

clues: Users are seeing a security warning when visiting the company's website.
The site loads if the user clicks 'Advanced' and 'Proceed anyway'.
The issue started occurring for everyone simultaneously today.
The server is still perfectly reachable via HTTP or direct IP.
Clicking the padlock icon in the browser shows 'Certificate is not valid'.
explanation: A sudden, universal privacy warning where the browser explicitly states the certificate is invalid almost always means the SSL/TLS certificate has passed its expiration date.
fixSteps: Generate a new CSR (Certificate Signing Request) on the web server
Purchase or renew the certificate with your Certificate Authority (CA)
Install the new certificate on the server and restart the web services

clues: A user can't save their current work and receives notifications about low system resources.
Windows updates fail to install with error code 0x80070070.
The system tray occasionally pops up a balloon notification about low resources.
Virtual memory paging is failing, causing extreme system sluggishness.
Opening File Explorer reveals the C: drive bar is completely red with 0 bytes free.
explanation: The inability to save files, failing updates with 0x80070070, and a red bar in File Explorer unambiguously confirm that the storage drive has completely run out of capacity.
fixSteps: Use the built-in Disk Cleanup tool to remove temporary files and old Windows update caches
Empty the Recycle Bin and check the Downloads folder for large, unneeded files
Consider migrating data to a secondary drive, network share, or upgrading the primary drive

clues: Devices throughout the office randomly lose their network connection several times a day.
A simple power cycle of the wireless router temporarily resolves the issue.
Wired Ethernet devices connected to the same router never experience drops.
Signal strength is excellent, and there is no microwave or structural interference.
The manufacturer's support forum is flooded with complaints about the same model since last month's automatic update.
explanation: When only wireless clients drop, interference is ruled out, reboots temporarily fix it, and a recent auto-update coincides with widespread community complaints, a firmware defect is the culprit.
fixSteps: Check the manufacturer's website for a newer hotfix firmware and apply it
If no fix is available, flash the router back to the previous stable firmware version
Disable automatic firmware updates until the issue is officially resolved

clues: Coworkers are receiving strange messages from a colleague asking for favors.
The user finds legitimate emails automatically moved to the 'Deleted Items' folder.
Login history shows successful authentications from overseas IP addresses.
The user admits to clicking a link in a 'password expiry' email yesterday.
A new forwarding rule was found in the mailbox settings sending all mail to an external address.
explanation: Unexpected forwarding rules, deleted emails, overseas logins, and sending scams after clicking a suspicious link are the hallmarks of a Business Email Compromise (BEC) via phishing.
fixSteps: Immediately reset the user's password and terminate all active login sessions
Enable or enforce Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on the account
Remove all unauthorized inbox rules and scan the local machine for malware

clues: A computer that rebooted for maintenance last night now won't start properly.
The system now repeatedly crashes during the boot sequence.
A Blue Screen of Death shows error code 'INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE'.
Startup Repair runs automatically but says it 'couldn't repair your PC'.
Rolling back the system using a restore point instantly resolves the issue.
explanation: When a machine loops during boot immediately following a scheduled update, and system restore fixes it, the update payload itself was defective or conflicted with system drivers.
fixSteps: Boot into the Windows Recovery Environment (WinRE)
Uninstall the latest Quality or Feature update
Pause Windows Updates temporarily until Microsoft releases a patch

clues: After logging in, the desktop looks completely different from how it was yesterday.
All their desktop shortcuts and pinned taskbar icons are missing.
A balloon notification says 'You've been signed in with a temporary profile'.
Any files saved to the desktop during this session disappear after a reboot.
Checking C:\Users\ shows a folder named 'username.000' or 'TEMP'.
explanation: Windows falls back to a temporary profile (which discards data on logout) when it cannot read the NTUSER.DAT registry hive of the original profile.
fixSteps: Open the Registry Editor and navigate to ProfileList under HKLM
Delete the temporary profile key and remove the '.bak' extension from the correct profile key
If the issue persists, create a brand new local user account and migrate the data

clues: Two employees working next to each other keep losing network access at different times.
When one user unplugs their Ethernet, the other user's connection instantly stabilizes.
Windows periodically shows a warning balloon near the system clock.
The warning explicitly states 'Windows has detected an IP address conflict'.
One of the users manually set their adapter settings to bypass the DHCP server.
explanation: When two devices on the same subnet claim the same IP address, routing breaks for both as packets are sent to the wrong MAC addresses interchangeably.
fixSteps: Change the offending client's IPv4 configuration back to 'Obtain an IP address automatically'
Release and renew the IP lease via command prompt (ipconfig /renew)
Verify the manually assigned IP is excluded from the DHCP scope

clues: A computer that was working fine yesterday now shows no signs of life when powered on.
The fans spin up to maximum RPM and stay there.
There are no POST (Power-On Self-Test) diagnostic beeps from the internal speaker.
The keyboard's Caps Lock and Num Lock lights do not toggle when pressed.
Swapping the power supply, RAM, and GPU yields the exact same dead state.
explanation: When a system receives power (fans spin) but fails to initialize the BIOS/UEFI (no POST beeps, no USB peripheral initialization) despite known-good components, the mainboard logic has failed.
fixSteps: Perform a bare-bench test to rule out short circuits from the PC case
Clear the CMOS by removing the battery to reset BIOS defaults
Replace the motherboard as a last resort

clues: The entire office network has suddenly become unusable.
The activity lights on all network switches are blinking furiously and simultaneously.
Pinging the local router results in response times over 1000ms or 'Request timed out'.
A user admits they 'cleaned up the cables under their desk'.
Upon inspection, both ends of a single Ethernet cable are plugged into the same unmanaged switch.
explanation: Plugging a switch into itself creates a Layer 2 loop. Without spanning-tree protocol (STP), broadcast frames circulate endlessly, multiplying until they consume 100% of the network bandwidth.
fixSteps: Physically trace and unplug the looped Ethernet cable to break the storm
Power cycle the affected switches to clear the saturated MAC address tables
Replace unmanaged desktop switches with managed switches that enforce Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)

clues: A user reports that they can't open any of their important work files.
The file icons have changed to blank white pages.
Every file extension has been appended with something like '.locked' or '.crypt'.
A text file named 'READ_ME_NOW.txt' has appeared in every single folder.
The text file demands payment in Bitcoin to restore access to the data.
explanation: The mass encryption of files combined with a demand for cryptocurrency is the definitive signature of a ransomware infection.
fixSteps: Physically disconnect the infected computer from the network to prevent lateral movement
Do not pay the ransom, as decryption is never guaranteed
Wipe the drive completely and restore the files from an offline, uninfected backup

clues: A desktop computer randomly turns off without warning.
The shutdowns happen most frequently when launching 3D games or rendering video.
The system will not turn back on unless the user flips the switch on the back of the case off and on again.
Temperatures for the CPU and GPU remain well within normal limits.
The user mentions smelling a faint 'burning electronics' odor near the back vent.
explanation: Sudden hard power-offs under heavy graphical load (high wattage draw) coupled with requiring a physical switch reset indicates the power supply's over-current protection is tripping or the unit is dying.
fixSteps: Immediately stop using the PC to prevent the failing unit from surging and killing other components
Test the unit with a PSU tester to verify voltage drops on the 12V rail
Replace the power supply with a high-quality unit of adequate wattage

clues: The monitor screen is black but the computer is clearly powered on and running.
The PC tower is clearly powered on and you can hear the Windows login sound.
The monitor briefly displays a floating 'No Signal' box before going to sleep.
The user recently moved their desk to clean behind it.
Pushing the connector firmly into the back of the monitor instantly brings the picture back.
explanation: A 'No Signal' message when the PC is definitively running (audio plays) means the monitor is working but receiving no data payload, usually due to a disconnected or partially seated cable.
fixSteps: Verify the cable is securely seated at both the monitor and PC ends
Ensure the cable is plugged into the dedicated graphics card, not the disabled motherboard port
Replace the cable if the connector pins are visibly bent

clues: A user has completely lost access to the network and internet.
The network icon in the system tray shows a globe with a 'not connected' symbol.
Windows network troubleshooter says 'A network cable is not properly plugged in or may be broken'.
There are no blinking link lights on the back of the PC's network card.
The user admits their dog was under the desk earlier playing with toys.
explanation: The complete absence of link lights combined with the OS explicitly stating the cable is unplugged means Layer 1 (Physical) connectivity has been severed.
fixSteps: Check the back of the PC and push the RJ45 connector until it clicks
Trace the cable to the wall jack and ensure it is firmly seated there
Test with a known-good patch cable if physical damage is suspected

clues: An employee can't log into their computer on Monday morning.
The user admits they forgot their password and tried guessing it 5 times.
The Windows logon screen explicitly says 'The referenced account is currently locked out'.
The user has the correct password now but it still won't accept it.
The Active Directory console shows a red 'X' over the user's computer icon.
explanation: Security policies automatically disable authentication for an account after a threshold of invalid attempts to prevent brute-force credential stuffing.
fixSteps: Verify the user's identity before proceeding
Open Active Directory Users and Computers (ADUC)
Uncheck the 'Account is locked out' box in the user's account properties

clues: A specific program slows down significantly after being open for a few hours.
Closing the application and reopening it temporarily restores performance to normal.
Opening Task Manager reveals this single application is consuming 24GB of RAM.
The RAM usage slowly but continuously climbs the longer the app is left open.
The developer recently released a patch that introduced a new background syncing feature.
explanation: When an application claims system memory for tasks but fails to release it back to the OS upon completion, usage infinitely inflates until the system runs out of resources.
fixSteps: Establish a temporary workaround by having the user restart the app periodically
Capture application logs and memory dumps when the usage is heavily bloated
Escalate to the software vendor or development team to patch the bad code

clues: A remote employee can't access any company systems from home.
Their home internet is working perfectly fine for Netflix and Zoom.
The Cisco AnyConnect client stalls at 'Authenticating...'.
The client eventually pops up an error stating 'Authentication failed due to problem with credentials'.
The user recently got a new smartphone and didn't migrate their Authenticator app.
explanation: If a remote user has internet but cannot establish a tunnel specifically because of credential rejection, and they lost their MFA token generator, the authentication handshake is failing.
fixSteps: Verify the user's Active Directory password hasn't expired
Reset the user's MFA bindings in the identity provider portal (e.g. Duo, Azure AD)
Walk the user through registering their new mobile device for 2FA

clues: A newly installed design application runs with very poor performance.
The PC meets all the recommended hardware requirements for the software.
Windows Update claims 'You're up to date'.
Device Manager shows the GPU driver is from 2019.
The software vendor's documentation states the app requires OpenGL 4.6 support.
explanation: Newer intensive software often relies on API optimizations (DirectX/OpenGL) that are only bundled in recent graphics driver releases from the GPU manufacturer.
fixSteps: Download the latest driver package directly from NVIDIA, AMD, or Intel's website
Run the installer and perform a 'Clean Installation' if offered
Reboot the workstation and test the application performance

clues: Every time the user turns on the PC, the system clock says it is January 1st, 2012.
Websites refuse to load, throwing 'SSL Certificate Date Invalid' errors in the browser.
The computer halts during boot with a 'Press F1 to Run SETUP' message.
The issue only occurs after the desktop has been unplugged from the wall overnight.
The PC is a legacy workstation that is over 8 years old.
explanation: The CR2032 battery on the motherboard is responsible for retaining BIOS settings and the Real-Time Clock (RTC) when external power is cut. A dead battery causes complete settings amnesia.
fixSteps: Purchase a standard CR2032 lithium coin cell battery
Open the PC chassis and swap the battery on the motherboard
Boot into BIOS, set the correct date and time, and save changes

clues: A group of users in one area of the office suddenly can't access network resources.
Running 'ipconfig' shows their IP address is 192.168.0.x instead of the corporate 10.0.0.x schema.
The default gateway is listed as a consumer-grade router IP (192.168.0.1).
A user brought a personal Wi-Fi router from home to 'boost the signal in their cubicle'.
They plugged the corporate wall jack into the 'LAN' port of their home router.
explanation: Plugging a consumer router's LAN port into an enterprise network injects a secondary DHCP server into the broadcast domain. Client devices will accept whichever IP lease responds fastest, breaking their routing.
fixSteps: Locate and immediately disconnect the unauthorized consumer router
Have affected users release and renew their IP addresses to get back on the corporate scope
Enable DHCP Snooping on the enterprise switches to block unauthorized DHCP offers

clues: An employee's phone has stopped receiving work emails and calendar notifications.
The Outlook app displays a banner saying 'Your device is non-compliant'.
The user admits they recently 'jailbroke' their phone to install custom themes.
The corporate Intune portal shows the device status as 'Quarantined'.
Security policy mandates that compromised OS kernels lose access to company data.
explanation: Mobile Device Management (MDM) platforms actively monitor device health. Jailbroken or rooted devices inherently bypass OS security models, triggering automated compliance blocks.
fixSteps: Inform the user that jailbroken devices are strictly prohibited by company policy
Instruct the user to factory reset the device and install the official OS firmware
Re-enroll the cleaned device into the MDM platform

clues: Outlook freezes whenever trying to access older archived emails.
An error pops up saying 'The file archive.pst cannot be opened'.
The local archive file has ballooned to over 48 GB in size.
Other mailboxes and incoming emails work perfectly fine.
The file is stored on a mechanical hard drive that was unexpectedly powered off yesterday.
explanation: PST files are databases. When they exceed Microsoft's recommended size limits (50GB) or suffer an abrupt power loss during a write operation, the database index shatters.
fixSteps: Close Outlook completely from the Task Manager
Run the built-in Inbox Repair Tool (SCANPST.EXE) on the affected file
If repaired, immediately archive old emails to reduce the file size, or migrate to Exchange Online Archiving

clues: The computer displays a warning on a black screen and refuses to proceed with booting.
The computer refuses to boot past the BIOS logo.
Looking through the side panel, the cooler on top of the processor is completely stationary.
The fan blades are visibly caked in thick, grey dust.
Flicking the fan blade with a pen reveals it is completely seized up and won't spin.
explanation: Motherboards mandate a tachometer reading from the CPU_FAN header to prevent the processor from melting down. A physically seized fan bearing triggers this hard halt.
fixSteps: Power down the machine and unplug it from the wall
Unclip the broken fan from the heatsink and replace it with a new fan of identical size
Plug the new fan into the CPU_FAN header on the motherboard

clues: An employee returning from vacation cannot log into their computer and is prompted to take an action.
The login screen shakes and rejects their password.
A small message under the password box reads 'Your password has expired and must be changed'.
The user admits they ignored the 'Your password expires in 3 days' balloon notifications.
The corporate policy mandates a password rotation every 90 days.
explanation: When the 90-day Kerberos ticket lifespan is exceeded, Active Directory rejects the old hash and requires the client to supply a new password before granting access.
fixSteps: Instruct the user to use the 'Reset Password' link on the login screen if Self-Service is enabled
Otherwise, verify their identity and reset the password manually in Active Directory
Ensure the user checks 'Require password change at next logon'

clues: The laptop keyboard is typing random characters and the touchpad is behaving erratically.
The trackpad behaves erratically, randomly clicking and dragging files.
When lifting the laptop, a sticky, dark substance drips from the bottom vents.
The laptop smells strongly of roasted coffee.
The user sheepishly asks if 'rice actually works'.
explanation: Ghost inputs across multiple keys (matrix shorting) combined with sticky residue and coffee smells are irrefutable evidence of a liquid spill bridging the electrical contacts.
fixSteps: Immediately hold the power button to force a shutdown and unplug the battery
Do not attempt to turn the device back on; rice does not clean conductive residue
Dismantle the device, clean components with 99% isopropyl alcohol, or replace the entire keyboard assembly

clues: A newly installed processor isn't recognized by the computer even though it's physically compatible.
The system powers on, but the screen remains black with no POST.
Putting the old, previous-generation CPU back in makes the system work perfectly.
The motherboard box says 'Ryzen 5000 Series Ready' but the new CPU is a 5800X3D released later.
The motherboard firmware version in the setup menu is from 3 years ago.
explanation: Motherboards physically compatible with newer CPUs cannot initialize them if the microcode instructions aren't present in the current BIOS ROM.
fixSteps: Install the old, working CPU so the system can POST
Download the latest BIOS firmware from the manufacturer's website to a USB drive
Use the motherboard's built-in flash utility to update the BIOS, then swap in the new CPU

clues: A visitor's device connects to the Wi-Fi but nothing loads.
Their laptop authenticates successfully but fails to pull an IP address.
Other existing devices connect and get IP addresses instantly.
The guest's laptop works perfectly on the open 'Guest' network.
The network administrator mentions a strict 'whitelist-only' policy for the secure SSID.
explanation: When authentication (WPA2) succeeds but DHCP allocation is denied exclusively for new devices, the router is employing MAC address filtering, silently dropping traffic from unapproved hardware addresses.
fixSteps: Find the physical MAC address (Physical Address) of the guest's wireless adapter
Log into the wireless controller or router dashboard
Add the guest's MAC address to the approved whitelist

clues: Several specific keys on the keyboard have stopped producing any characters when pressed.
All other keys on the keyboard work perfectly fine.
The user confirms they spilled a small amount of soda on that exact area of the keyboard last week.
The on-screen keyboard app works fine as a workaround.
Plugging in a different USB keyboard resolves all issues.

fixSteps: Unplug the keyboard and place it upside down on a towel to drain any residual liquid

clues: Audio from the laptop sounds distorted and crackly, especially on lower frequencies.
Headphones plugged into the same headphone jack produce crystal clear audio.
The sound is described as a 'buzzing' or 'rattling' quality on bass notes.
The issue started after a particularly loud video conference call last week.
External Bluetooth speakers connected to the laptop have no audio issues.
explanation: Clear headphone and Bluetooth output but distorted internal audio indicates the physical speaker drivers have been mechanically overdriven or damaged (blown).
fixSteps: Use headphones or external speakers as an immediate workaround
In Windows Sound settings, ensure audio enhancements are disabled
Replace the internal speaker assembly if the device is under warranty

clues: The computer shuts down after a short time playing games and the graphics card is extremely hot.
The graphics card temperature in MSI Afterburner reaches 92°C before shutdown.
The GPU fans audibly spin up but then stop periodically during gaming.
The user has not cleaned the inside of their computer case in over 2 years.
Opening the side panel reveals a thick layer of dust caked on the GPU heatsink fins.
explanation: Dust accumulation on the GPU heatsink thermally insulates the fins and prevents proper airflow, causing the graphics card to hit thermal limit and trigger emergency shutdown.
fixSteps: Power down and ground yourself before opening the case
Use compressed air to thoroughly clean the GPU heatsink fins and fan blades
Monitor temperatures afterward to confirm the issue is resolved

clues: A newly assembled custom computer won't power on despite all components being correctly installed.
The power supply fan spins briefly when the switch is turned on, then stops.
The motherboard's diagnostic LEDs flash for a split second then go dark.
The user admits this is their first PC build and they 'winged it'.
The motherboard is sitting directly on the metal case panel without any raised mounts underneath.
explanation: Without standoffs, the motherboard's solder points contact the metal case directly, creating a short circuit that triggers the power supply's over-current protection (OCP) almost instantly.
fixSteps: Remove the motherboard from the case entirely
Install the brass standoffs in the correct ATX mounting holes on the case tray
Reinstall the motherboard so it sits elevated above the case metal

clues: An all-in-one computer intermittently loses power and goes dark for no apparent reason.
Wiggling the power cable near the power brick connection makes the screen flicker.
The power adapter's LED indicator goes off when the screen goes black.
The monitor works fine when plugged into a different power outlet in the office.
A known-good power cable from another monitor does not exhibit this behavior.
explanation: An intermittent connection within the power cable (likely a broken conductor near the connector boot) causes the PSU to lose AC input when the cable is moved, which is classic for a cable that has been kinked or strained.
fixSteps: Replace the standard IEC C13 power cable with a known-good spare
If the cable is integrated, replace the entire power brick assembly
Route the new cable to avoid sharp bends or being pinched by furniture

clues: The mouse and keyboard plugged into the front of a computer have stopped responding.
Both devices are plugged into the front USB ports of the PC case.
The rear USB ports on the motherboard work perfectly with the same devices.
Windows plays the 'device disconnect' sound repeatedly every few seconds.
In Device Manager, a yellow triangle appears on 'Unknown USB Device (Port Reset Failed)'.
explanation: Front USB ports fail when the internal header cable becomes loose, is pinched, or when the cheap header pins on the motherboard crack. The constant disconnect sound indicates electrical instability.
fixSteps: Check the internal USB 2.0/3.0 header connection on the motherboard for looseness
Inspect the front panel header cable for visible damage (pinched or cut wires)
If the header is damaged, use a PCIe USB expansion card as a permanent solution

clues: A computer connected to a backup power supply shut down during a brief power flicker.
The UPS unit emits a continuous beeping sound and will not power on.
Pressing the 'Test' button on the UPS produces no audible click or fan spin.
The UPS is 5 years old and has been through many power outages.
Opening the battery compartment reveals visible corrosion and swelling around the battery terminals.
explanation: A UPS that powers equipment from AC line-in but fails during outage has lost its DC battery capacity. Swollen, corroded terminals confirm the lead-acid battery has expired (typical 3-5 year lifespan).
fixSteps: Immediately unplug the UPS from AC power and the PC
Replace the internal battery with the manufacturer's specified replacement part number
Perform a runtime calibration test after the new battery is installed

clues: A desktop PC powers on with fans spinning and lights on, but no display output.
There are zero POST beeps from the internal speaker, even though one is connected.
The keyboard and mouse LEDs do not light up during boot.
Reseating the RAM and GPU does not change anything.
The system will only boot if a single RAM stick is installed in the second slot.
explanation: Zero POST beeps and no peripheral initialization when both RAM sticks are installed, but booting with one stick in a specific slot, indicates one memory channel on the CPU or motherboard has physically failed.
fixSteps: Test each RAM slot individually with a known-good stick to confirm which channel is dead
Update the motherboard BIOS in case it's a memory compatibility issue
If confirmed, operate in single-channel mode or replace the motherboard

clues: A secondary storage drive randomly disappears from the file list and then reappears after reboot.
The drive re-appears after a full system shutdown and cold boot.
Disk Management shows the drive as 'Unknown, Not Initialized' when it disappears.
SMART diagnostics on the drive itself report no errors or bad sectors.
Bumping the desk slightly causes the drive to immediately disconnect.
explanation: A failing SATA data cable with loose or corroded pins causes intermittent connectivity. The drive drops from the SATA bus and the OS treats it as a removed device, restoring only after a full bus re-enumeration on cold boot.
fixSteps: Power down the PC and reseat both ends of the SATA data cable firmly
If the issue persists, replace the SATA cable entirely with a known-good cable
Consider using a locking-latch SATA cable for a more secure connection

clues: Random colored shapes and patterns appear on screen when running any visually intensive task.
The artifacts are still visible in the BIOS screen and Windows boot logo.
Temperatures for the graphics card remain under 70°C.
Reinstalling graphics drivers and rolling back to older versions does not help.
The GPU has been overclocked with a custom voltage curve for the past year.
explanation: Artifacts visible even in POST screen and unaffected by driver changes point squarely to physical VRAM failure. Overclocking accelerates memory chip degradation when voltage tolerances are exceeded over time.
fixSteps: Reduce the GPU memory clock to stock or below-stock speeds to test for stability
Run a VRAM stress test (e.g., OCCT VRAM test or MemTestCL) to identify failed memory chips
If under warranty, RMA the graphics card; otherwise, consider underclocking as a temporary mitigation

clues: The laptop's wireless connection drops every few minutes and requires a reboot to reconnect.
The Wi-Fi adapter disappears from Device Manager when the issue occurs.
Reinstalling drivers and resetting network settings does not resolve the problem.
A USB Wi-Fi dongle works perfectly in the same location without any drops.
The laptop model is known for having defective Intel 9560 Wi-Fi cards from that production batch.
explanation: The Wi-Fi adapter vanishing from Device Manager is a hardware-level failure, not a driver issue. Known batch defects in integrated Wi-Fi modules cause them to thermally fail after short periods of operation.
fixSteps: Open Device Manager and check if the Wi-Fi adapter appears when hidden devices are shown
Replace the internal M.2 Wi-Fi card with a compatible replacement module
If replacement is not possible, use a high-quality USB Wi-Fi adapter as a permanent solution

clues: A newly installed expansion card is not detected by the system despite being properly seated.
The new GPU's fans spin, but there is no display output.
Plugging the old GPU back into the same slot also fails to produce display output.
The new GPU works perfectly in a different computer.
Inspecting the PCIe slot reveals two bent pins inside the slot.
explanation: Bent pins inside the PCIe slot prevent proper electrical contact between the motherboard and the expansion card. Physical damage usually occurs from uneven insertion force or improper card removal.
fixSteps: Try the expansion card in a different PCIe slot on the same motherboard
If a second x16 slot exists, use that permanently (may run at x8 speed, which is negligible for most GPUs)
If no other slot is available, replace the motherboard

clues: A computer that was working fine suddenly refuses to boot showing only a black screen and cursor.
The drive is not listed in the BIOS boot priority menu at all.
When the PC does manage to start, the drive appears as 'RAW' in Disk Management.
The M.2 drive gets excessively hot to the touch even when idle.
The error 'A device which does not exist was specified' appears in Event Viewer.
explanation: An NVMe drive appearing as RAW with overheating symptoms indicates the controller chip has failed. M.2 drives are particularly susceptible to thermal damage in poorly ventilated laptop chassis.
fixSteps: Try reseating the M.2 drive in its slot to rule out connection issues
Attempt data recovery by connecting it via an external NVMe enclosure to another PC
Replace the failed SSD and restore from backup

clues: The computer case emits an unusual noise that has gotten louder over the past few days.
The noise is described as a grinding or rattling sound coming from inside the tower.
The sound changes pitch when the computer is moved or tilted slightly.
Opening the side panel reveals one of the case fans is wobbling as it spins.
Tapping the center of the wobbling fan temporarily changes the sound.
explanation: A grinding noise from a wobbling fan that changes with movement indicates the fan's sleeve or ball bearing has worn out, causing the blade hub to contact the fan housing.
fixSteps: Identify which fan is failing by stopping each fan momentarily with a non-conductive object
Replace the faulty fan with a new matching-size fan
Route the new fan cable neatly and secure it to prevent it from contacting the blades

clues: The laptop's built-in pointing device has become unreliable and jumps around the screen.
The cursor occasionally moves in the opposite direction from finger movement.
The touchpad works fine after a reboot but deteriorates after a few hours of use.
An external mouse connected to the laptop works perfectly without any issues.
Pressing on the touchpad surface reveals a swollen area underneath the palm rest.
explanation: The laptop's internal battery swelling beneath the touchpad area puts upward pressure on the touchpad controller, causing erratic cursor behavior. The condition is also a fire safety risk.
fixSteps: Immediately power down the laptop and stop using it to prevent further battery damage
Open the chassis and inspect the battery for visible swelling or deformation
Replace the swollen battery with a factory-authorized replacement

clues: Other participants in video calls cannot hear the user speak.
The user can hear everyone else perfectly on the call.
The microphone icon in the call application shows no input activity when speaking.
Testing the microphone in Windows Sound settings shows no bars moving.
A USB headset microphone works fine when plugged into the same computer.
explanation: When an external microphone works but the built-in one shows no signal at all, the internal microphone hardware has physically failed or the internal connection has become loose.
fixSteps: Check if the microphone is disabled in Device Manager or Sound settings
Ensure no privacy settings are blocking microphone access to applications
Use a USB or Bluetooth headset microphone as a permanent replacement for the failed internal unit

clues: The laptop screen is extremely dim and barely visible even at the brightest setting.
Shining a flashlight at the screen reveals that an image is actually being displayed very faintly.
Connecting an external monitor shows the desktop perfectly on the external display.
The screen brightness keys do not produce any visible change when pressed.
Windows reports the display brightness at 100% in the settings panel.
explanation: An image visible only under direct light with no brightness adjustment working indicates the LED backlight or its driver circuitry has failed while the LCD panel itself remains functional.
fixSteps: Use an external monitor as a temporary workaround
Check for a loose display cable by gently opening and closing the lid
Replace the entire display assembly as the backlight is typically integrated into the panel

clues: The computer turns off unexpectedly and immediately tries to restart in a loop.
Holding the power button for 10 seconds does not fully shut down the system.
The computer eventually boots but then shuts down again after a random interval.
The behavior started after a minor spill near the power button area.
Disconnecting the front panel power button header and shorting the pins manually works normally.
explanation: A stuck or shorted power button sends a continuous 'press' signal to the motherboard. The system interprets this as repeated power commands causing unwanted shutdowns and immediate restart attempts.
fixSteps: Unplug the front panel power switch header from the motherboard
Clean the power button mechanism with isopropyl alcohol or replace the switch
If the switch is integrated into the case, reassign the reset button as the new power button

clues: A computer that previously felt very responsive now feels sluggish during normal operation.
The user reports that recently accessed files now take noticeably longer to load.
Disk activity is near 100% in Task Manager even when the computer is idle.
The system has an Intel Optane memory module configured to accelerate a mechanical hard drive.
The Optane memory utility reports the module status as 'detected but not in accelerated mode'.
explanation: When the Optane memory module fails or its driver becomes corrupted, the acceleration layer is lost and the system reverts to full mechanical drive performance, causing dramatic slowdowns.
fixSteps: Open the Intel Optane Memory application and check the module status
Try disabling and re-enabling Optane acceleration in the utility
If the module is physically dead, remove it and rely on the primary storage alone

clues: The computer emits a series of beeps and shows nothing on the screen when powered on.
The beep pattern is consistent with a memory initialization failure according to the motherboard manual.
The computer was recently moved to a new desk location.
Reseating the RAM sticks by removing and reinserting them does not initially resolve the issue.
After cleaning the RAM contacts with a pencil eraser and reseating, the computer boots normally.
explanation: Physical shock from moving a computer can partially dislodge RAM modules from their slots. Even a millimeter of gap can break the electrical connection, preventing POST from completing.
fixSteps: Completely remove and firmly reseat all memory modules until the retention clips click
Ensure the modules are inserted into the correct slots per the motherboard manual
Run a memory diagnostic after successful boot to verify no permanent damage occurred

clues: A desktop computer suddenly lost all wired network connectivity during a thunderstorm.
The network icon shows a red X indicating no connection.
The Ethernet port's link lights are completely off even with a known-good cable plugged in.
Device Manager shows the network adapter with a yellow exclamation mark and error 'Code 10'.
A USB-to-Ethernet adapter immediately restores network functionality.
explanation: A nearby lightning strike can induce a surge through Ethernet cabling that jumps the isolation barrier and destroys the physical layer transceiver on the motherboard's integrated NIC.
fixSteps: Check the Ethernet cable for damage and verify it works with another device
Install a USB-to-Ethernet adapter as a quick replacement for the dead port
Add surge protectors with Ethernet pass-through to prevent future occurrences

clues: The laptop cannot read any memory cards inserted into its built-in slot.
The card slot was working last week but stopped after a card was inserted with some force.
Windows makes the 'device connected' sound when a card is inserted but no drive letter appears.
Disk Management shows the card as 'Unknown' and 'Not Initialized' when inserted.
The same memory card works perfectly when using a USB card reader instead.
explanation: Forcing a memory card into a slot with delicate pins can bend or break the contacts, preventing reliable electrical connection even though the card physically fits.
fixSteps: Inspect the slot with a bright light and magnifier for bent pins
If pins are bent, attempt careful straightening with fine tweezers
If the slot is damaged beyond repair, use an external USB card reader permanently

clues: The computer boots up but the secondary storage drive is not visible anywhere.
The primary drive with the operating system works perfectly fine.
The missing drive was present and working yesterday before the computer was moved.
Disk Management shows no sign of the secondary drive in the list of connected disks.
Opening the case reveals the drive's data cable is hanging loose inside the chassis.
explanation: Movement during transport can cause internal SATA data or power cables to work loose from their connectors, removing the drive from the system entirely without any component failure.
fixSteps: Power down the computer and open the case
Firmly reseat both ends of the SATA data cable and the power connector
Secure cables with cable ties to prevent future disconnection

clues: The computer produces no sound from the rear audio jacks.
Front panel headphone jack also has no output.
Device Manager shows the audio device with a yellow exclamation mark.
Reinstalling audio drivers from the manufacturer website does not help.
A USB audio adapter plugged into any USB port produces clear sound immediately.
explanation: When no audio jacks work, driver reinstalls fail, and a USB adapter works, the motherboard's integrated audio codec chip has physically failed or its connection to the chipset is damaged.
fixSteps: Verify audio is not disabled in the BIOS settings
Use a USB audio adapter or USB headset as a permanent replacement
If multiple other components are failing, consider replacing the motherboard

clues: Several keys on the keyboard produce characters inconsistently or not at all.
The affected keys are the most commonly used letters in daily typing.
The keyboard is over three years old and heavily used.
The on-screen keyboard works perfectly well as an alternative.
Plugging in a different keyboard resolves the issue immediately.
explanation: Mechanical keyboard switches have a rated lifespan (typically 50-100 million keystrokes). Heavy daily use on common keys can wear out individual switch contacts causing intermittent or failed input.
fixSteps: Test the keyboard on another computer to confirm the hardware failure
Replace individual key switches if the keyboard has a hot-swappable design
If not serviceable, replace the entire keyboard

clues: The laptop works when plugged into the power adapter but the battery percentage stays the same.
The battery icon shows 'Plugged in, not charging' status.
Removing and reinserting the battery does not change the behavior.
The power adapter's LED is lit and the laptop recognizes it as connected.
BIOS reports the battery as present but at a fixed charge level that never changes.
explanation: Lithium-ion batteries have a finite number of charge cycles. When a battery reaches end-of-life, the internal battery management system (BMS) prevents charging for safety, showing 'Plugged in, not charging'.
fixSteps: Run a battery diagnostic report using `powercfg /batteryreport` in Command Prompt
If the battery health is critically low (below 20% design capacity), order a replacement battery
Replace the battery following the manufacturer's service manual

clues: The dedicated graphics card is not detected by the system at all.
The computer is using the integrated motherboard graphics instead.
The GPU fans do not spin when the computer is powered on.
The computer was recently transported to a LAN event.
Reseating the power cables to the graphics card makes it immediately detected on the next boot.
explanation: Modern graphics cards require dedicated PCIe power cables. If these become loose during transport, the GPU has no power and will not initialize, causing the system to fall back to integrated graphics.
fixSteps: Power down completely, unplug the power cord, and open the case
Firmly reseat the PCIe power cables at both the GPU end and the power supply end
If the issue persists, try different PCIe power cable ports on the power supply

clues: The external monitor connected to the laptop flickers or shows no signal intermittently.
The issue worsens when the desk is bumped or the laptop lid is adjusted.
The monitor works perfectly when connected to a different computer.
The laptop works perfectly when connected to a different monitor.
Replacing the cable between the laptop and monitor resolves the problem entirely.
explanation: HDMI cables contain multiple tiny conductors that can fracture from repeated bending at the connector stress point. A broken conductor causes intermittent or failed signal transmission.
fixSteps: Try a known-good HDMI cable to confirm the original cable is faulty
Inspect the original cable for kinks, sharp bends, or visible damage near the connectors
Replace with a high-quality, properly shielded HDMI cable

clues: Multiple devices on the same power strip stop working or behave erratically.
The surge protector's circuit breaker has tripped, or its protection light is off.
The power strip has been used for years and has many devices daisy-chained.
Plugging the devices directly into a wall outlet restores normal operation.
The surge protector feels warm to the touch even when powered off.
explanation: Surge protectors wear out over time as they absorb minor surges. An overloaded strip with daisy-chained devices can also exceed its rated capacity, causing the internal components to degrade or fail.
fixSteps: Unplug everything from the suspect power strip and test it with a simple desk lamp
If the power strip has failed, dispose of it properly and replace with a new unit
Ensure the replacement has adequate joule rating for the connected equipment

clues: The laptop does not recognize the external monitor dock when connected via USB-C.
Other USB-C accessories like flash drives still work in the same port.
The dock works perfectly on another computer with the same cable.
Inspecting the port reveals debris or slight damage inside the connector cavity.
Cleaning the port with compressed air does not resolve the issue.
explanation: Thunderbolt ports use additional high-speed signal lines beyond standard USB-C. Physical damage or contamination to the port can break the Thunderbolt alt-mode connection even while basic USB functionality remains.
fixSteps: Inspect the port carefully with a flashlight for bent pins or debris
Try a different USB-C cable and test the dock on the same port
If the port is physically damaged, use a different Thunderbolt port or a USB display adapter as a workaround

clues: The computer emits a high-pitched whining sound that varies with activity level.
The sound's pitch changes when the CPU is under load versus idle.
All case and CPU fans appear to spin normally when viewed through the side panel.
The noise is coming from the CPU cooler area specifically.
Software fan monitoring shows the CPU fan speed fluctuating wildly between 0 and 6000 RPM.
explanation: When a fan bearing wears out, the rotor can wobble at certain RPMs causing the blades to contact the housing or the bearing itself to produce audible vibration that changes with speed.
fixSteps: Stop the CPU fan momentarily with a non-conductive object to isolate the noise source
Replace the CPU cooler if the bearing is confirmed failing
Monitor CPU temperatures closely while waiting for the replacement cooler

clues: A newly installed M.2 NVMe drive is not detected by the computer.
The drive was installed correctly by following the motherboard manual.
The BIOS does not list the drive in any of the storage or boot menus.
The drive works perfectly when installed in another computer's M.2 slot.
Upon inspection, the drive is not lying flat in its slot and one end is raised.
explanation: M.2 drives must be secured at the mounting end with a screw. If missing, the drive can lift slightly from the connector, breaking electrical contact and preventing detection even though it appears inserted.
fixSteps: Power down and locate the M.2 mounting screw and standoff on the motherboard
If the standoff is missing, install one in the correct position for the drive length
Insert the drive at a 30-degree angle, press down, and secure with the mounting screw

clues: After a power outage, the computer boots to a message saying settings have been reset.
The date and time in the BIOS are showing the default manufacturer date.
The boot order has reverted to factory defaults instead of the configured SSD.
The system had custom fan curves and overclock settings that are now gone.
This computer is over five years old and this is the first time it lost settings.
explanation: The CR2032 coin cell that powers BIOS settings and the real-time clock has a 3-5 year lifespan. When it depletes, every full power loss wipes all customized BIOS configurations.
fixSteps: Replace the CR2032 coin cell battery on the motherboard
Re-enter all custom BIOS settings after battery replacement
Save the current BIOS configuration as a profile for quick restoration after future battery changes

clues: The computer suddenly stopped working and will not power on at all.
Nothing happens when the power button is pressed — no fans, no lights, no sound.
The power supply's fan also does not spin under any condition.
The computer was in use during an electrical storm when a bright flash occurred nearby.
Unplugging the power cord, waiting 30 seconds, and plugging it back in restores function.
explanation: Quality power supplies have surge protection circuitry that disconnects internal components when a dangerous voltage spike is detected. A full power drain resets the protection mechanism.
fixSteps: Perform a full power drain by unplugging and holding the power button for 30 seconds
If the system powers on after reconnection, test stability with a stress test
Consider replacing the PSU if this happens frequently, as it absorbed a significant surge

clues: The computer crashes with a blue screen showing 'IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL' error.
The crashes only happen when using a specific USB peripheral device.
The system works perfectly fine when the device is disconnected.
The error message in the blue screen mentions the file 'ntoskrnl.exe'.
The manufacturer of the USB device released a driver update specifically addressing BSOD issues.
explanation: The IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL stop code indicates a driver attempted to access a memory page at an inappropriate interrupt request level. A buggy USB driver accessing shared memory improperly is a common trigger.
fixSteps: Update the USB device driver from the manufacturer's official website
If no update exists, roll back the driver to a previous known-working version
As a last resort, uninstall the device driver completely and let Windows install the generic driver

clues: The screen goes black for a few seconds and then recovers with a notification about a driver issue.
The notification says the display driver has been stopped and successfully recovered.
This happens most frequently when watching videos or scrolling through image-heavy websites.
The GPU hardware acceleration feature is enabled in the web browser settings.
Disabling hardware acceleration in the browser completely stops the black screen episodes.
explanation: Windows' Timeout Detection and Recovery (TDR) mechanism restarts the GPU driver when it detects the graphics card hasn't responded within a few seconds. Hardware acceleration in browsers is a common trigger.
fixSteps: Update the graphics driver to the latest version from the GPU manufacturer
Disable hardware acceleration in web browsers and other applications
If the issue continues, reduce GPU clock speeds or increase the TDR timeout value in the registry

clues: A recently migrated website still loads the old version on the user's computer.
The old website loads on this computer but colleagues in other offices see the new site.
Clearing the browser cache does not resolve the issue.
The website loads correctly immediately when accessed from a different device on the same network.
Running a specific command from the command prompt resolves the issue instantly.
explanation: Windows caches DNS lookup results locally. When a website moves to a new server IP, the cached old IP persists until the TTL expires, causing the computer to resolve to the old location.
fixSteps: Open Command Prompt as Administrator and run 'ipconfig /flushdns'
Verify the correct IP resolves by running 'nslookup websitename.com'
If records are still stale, temporarily use a public DNS server like 8.8.8.8

clues: A user cannot connect their new laptop to the office Wi-Fi network.

explanation: Hidden networks (SSID broadcast disabled) require the exact network name including spaces and special characters. Newer Windows versions have stricter matching criteria that reject common typos or encoding differences.
fixSteps: Confirm the exact network name including capitalization and any special characters
Manually create a Wi-Fi profile with the precise SSID and security settings
Enable SSID broadcast on the access point if security concerns allow it

Determine the correct UNC path to the intended shared folder
Remap the drive letter to the correct share path

clues: A remote user can connect to the VPN but cannot access any internal company resources.

explanation: When a VPN client connects, it adds routes to push traffic through the tunnel. If these routes are stale or incorrect from a previous session, packets may be sent to the wrong gateway and dropped.

clues: The computer has become sluggish and some files fail to open with read errors.
Programs occasionally freeze while loading certain files.
Backing up files to an external drive fails partway through with an 'I/O device error'.
Running CHKDSK reports 'bad sectors' being found and marked.
The disk's S.M.A.R.T. status reports 'Reallocated Sector Count' has exceeded the warning threshold.
explanation: Hard drives develop bad sectors as the magnetic platters degrade. The drive firmware reallocates data from failing sectors to reserve space, but once the reserve is exhausted, read/write operations fail.
fixSteps: Immediately back up all critical data from the failing drive
Run CHKDSK /R to identify and mark bad sectors
Replace the drive and restore data from backup

clues: An external dock stopped working with the laptop after a system update.
The laptop charges through the dock's power delivery but no peripherals are detected.
Monitors, keyboard, and mouse connected to the dock all remain inactive.
The dock's USB ports work if the dock is plugged into a different computer.
Device Manager shows 'Unknown USB Device (Link In Reset)' when the dock is connected.
explanation: Windows updates can install generic Thunderbolt drivers that are incompatible with specific docking station firmware, breaking peripheral connectivity while power delivery still functions through the same cable.
fixSteps: Check Windows Update history for any Thunderbolt or chipset driver updates and roll them back
Install the latest Thunderbolt driver and firmware directly from the laptop manufacturer's support site
Power cycle the dock by unplugging it for 30 seconds to clear its internal state

clues: A new SSD is performing far slower than expected in benchmark tests.
The drive was cloned from an older mechanical hard drive.
The SSD's advertised speeds are over 500 MB/s but actual transfers are under 100 MB/s.
The drive is detected correctly and has plenty of free space.
The partition on the SSD starts at sector 63 instead of the modern alignment standard.
explanation: When cloning an old drive, the partition can be copied with legacy alignment (sector 63). Modern SSDs require 4K alignment for optimal performance, and misalignment causes partial write operations that drastically reduce speed.

clues: A large Excel workbook takes several minutes to recalculate after any change.

explanation: Volatile functions in Excel recalculate every time any change is made anywhere in the workbook, even if their dependencies haven't changed. Hundreds of volatile formulas can multiply calculation time dramatically.
fixSteps: Replace volatile functions (NOW, TODAY, INDIRECT, OFFSET, RAND) with non-volatile alternatives where possible
Set Excel calculation to manual (Formulas > Calculation Options > Manual) for editing sessions
Use iterative calculation settings to limit the number of recalculation passes

fixSteps: Restart the Task Scheduler service from Services.msc (right-click > Restart)

If tasks still don't run, export the task list, stop the service, clear the task cache files, restart the service, and import tasks back

clues: System Restore cannot find any restore points to use for recovery.

clues: Several Windows computers in the IT department are not applying the latest group policy changes.

answer: stale DNS cache with long TTL
aliases: dns not updated after server migration
explanation: DNS records have a Time-To-Live (TTL) value that determines how long resolvers cache the record. If the original DNS records had a long TTL, client machines and intermediate DNS resolvers may cache the old IP address for up to 24 hours after the migration, directing traffic to the old servers.
fixSteps: Reduce the TTL on the affected DNS records to 300 seconds (5 minutes) before future migrations to minimize cache impact
On affected client machines, run 'ipconfig /flushdns' from an administrator command prompt to clear the local DNS cache
If the issue persists, instruct users in the office to wait for the TTL to expire or restart their machines to clear the resolver cache

explanation: Applications that store session data in server-local memory require session affinity (sticky sessions) so that all requests from a user go to the same backend server. fixSteps: Access the load balancer management interface and locate the virtual server configuration for the web application

clues: SSH authentication using a key file fails with 'Permissions 0644 for private key are too open'.

explanation: OpenSSH security requires private key files to be readable only by the file owner. If group or other users have any access, SSH refuses to use the key to prevent potential credential theft by other processes or users.
fixSteps: Navigate to the .ssh directory containing the key file
Set the correct permissions by running 'icacls id_rsa /inheritance:r /grant "%USERNAME%":R' in Command Prompt

fixSteps: Open all printer access panels and carefully inspect the sensor area indicated in the error log
Remove any debris, torn paper fragments, or foreign objects from the sensor mechanism

clues: The monitor does not wake from sleep when the computer is activated.

explanation: Some monitors have difficulty detecting the video signal after the computer sends the display into sleep mode. The monitor stays in a low-power state because it doesn't recognize the signal restoration from the graphics card.
fixSteps: Try pressing the monitor's power button or input source button to force it to search for a signal

clues: A computer connected to the corporate network is treated as a 'Public' network instead of 'Domain'.

explanation: Windows determines the network profile (Domain, Private, or Public) based on the Network Location Awareness service detecting a domain controller. If NLA fails to detect the domain, the network defaults to 'Public', applying restrictive firewall rules.
fixSteps: Run 'gpupdate /force' to refresh Group Policy settings and domain network detection
Restart the Network Location Awareness service in Services.msc
If the issue persists, check that the network adapter's DNS suffix matches the domain DNS suffix

clues: A virtual machine is running very slowly and applications are timing out.

explanation: Virtual machine disk files can grow to fill available host storage, especially if log files inside the VM are not managed. When the host runs out of space, all VMs on that host may slow down, freeze, or crash due to storage I/O failures.
fixSteps: Connect to the VM and clean up unnecessary files, especially large log files
Compact the VHDX file using Hyper-V Manager or PowerShell to release unused space
Add additional storage to the host or enable storage QoS limits to prevent future overconsumption

fixSteps: Install the latest Windows updates on the client computer to update its RDP client

For the most secure solution, ensure all RDP clients are fully updated to the latest Windows version

clues: A Windows Failover Cluster has stopped serving applications and shows 'Offline' for all roles.

explanation: Windows Failover Clusters use a quorum mechanism to prevent split-brain scenarios. When a network disruption isolates nodes such that no group has a majority, the cluster loses quorum and all cluster services are taken offline to prevent data corruption.
fixSteps: Verify all cluster nodes have network connectivity between each other
Check the cluster event logs for the exact reason the quorum was lost
Once connectivity is restored, the cluster should automatically regain quorum; if not, force quorum from the node with the most current data

clues: Multiple computers in the office show different times from each other.

explanation: Active Directory requires time synchronization within 5 minutes between domain members. When the domain controller loses its NTP source, all domain members drift, causing Kerberos authentication failures because the ticket timestamps are outside the acceptable skew window.
fixSteps: On the domain controller, run 'w32tm /query /source' to check the current time source

clues: The laptop will not resume from hibernation and shows a blank screen.

explanation: Windows saves the system state to the hibernation file (hiberfil.sys) when entering hibernation. If this file becomes corrupted or too small to store the session (especially when RAM-intensive applications are running), the system cannot resume and must reboot.
fixSteps: Force a shutdown by holding the power button, then boot normally

clues: A user accessing a web application intermittently sees different session states.

fixSteps: Check the load balancer's persistence configuration method (source IP, cookie, SSL session ID)
Configure cookie-based persistence for web applications to maintain sessions regardless of client IP changes

explanation: Windows power plans can configure PCIe Link State Power Management to reduce power consumption. 'Maximum Power Savings' puts the PCIe bus (including Thunderbolt) into a low-power state that significantly reduces available bandwidth to external devices like eGPUs.

fixSteps: Check the status bar for 'Circular References' and click to navigate to the problematic cell

clues: A Linux server cannot write to an NFS-mounted directory that is shared from a NAS device.

aliases: linux cannot write to nfs share
explanation: NFS exports can use 'root_squash' which maps root user requests to the anonymous 'nobody' user. If the remote directory doesn't have write permissions for 'nobody', write operations fail even though the mount succeeded and reads are working.
fixSteps: Check the NFS export options on the server for 'root_squash' or 'all_squash' settings

clues: A scheduled backup task is running at the wrong time of day.

aliases: windows task scheduler utc vs local
explanation: Windows Task Scheduler triggers can be configured to use either local time or UTC. If the trigger time is set in UTC but the server is in a different time zone, the task runs at the UTC equivalent of the configured time.
fixSteps: Open Task Scheduler and locate the affected task
Edit the task trigger and check the 'Synchronize across time zones' setting

clues: An external USB drive is detected by the computer but shows as 'Unallocated Space' in Disk Management.
The drive has been working fine for years and was not formatted or manipulated recently.
The drive was ejected improperly during a file transfer.
Disk Management shows the drive as 'Unknown, Not Initialized'.
Data recovery software can still see the old partition structure and files.
explanation: The partition table (MBR or GPT) stores the layout of partitions on a drive. Improper ejection during a write operation can corrupt this table, causing the OS to see the drive as unallocated space even though the data is still physically present on the disk.
fixSteps: Do not initialize or format the drive as this will overwrite the existing data
Use a data recovery tool like TestDisk to scan for and restore the lost partition table
After recovering the partition, immediately back up all data and replace the drive if it is prone to corruption

clues: An external USB drive frequently reports 'Delayed Write Failed' error messages.

answer: USB power insufficient for disk write cache

explanation: External hard drives require stable power for the write cache to function. When connected through an unpowered USB hub that is sharing power among multiple devices, voltage drops can cause write cache failures and data corruption.
fixSteps: Connect the external drive directly to a USB port on the computer, not through a hub

clues: A Docker containerized application is failing with authentication errors.
The errors suggest the authentication tokens are being rejected as expired.
The host system clock is correct and synchronized with an NTP server.
The container's system clock is several minutes behind the host's clock.
Docker containers do not automatically synchronize time with the host.
explanation: Docker containers share the host's kernel but not the system clock directly. Over time, the container's clock can drift from the host's clock, causing time-sensitive operations like token validation to fail if the time difference exceeds the allowed skew.
fixSteps: Restart the container to reset its time or run 'docker exec -it [container] date -s "$(date -u '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')"'
Mount the host's /etc/localtime as a volume in the container to keep clocks in sync
For persistent time sync, add the host's time socket as a volume mount: '-v /etc/timezone:/etc/timezone:ro -v /etc/localtime:/etc/localtime:ro'

clues: A renewed SSL certificate is not working on the web server after installation.

explanation: SSL/TLS certificates cover specific domain names listed in the Subject Alternative Names (SANs) field. If the CSR was generated with an incomplete list of SANs, the issued certificate will not be trusted for the missing domains.
fixSteps: Generate a new CSR with the complete list of all domain names that need to be covered by the certificate
Submit the corrected CSR to the Certificate Authority for reissuance
Install the new certificate and verify it covers all required domains using an SSL checker tool

clues: A BIOS update on a BitLocker-encrypted computer failed to install.

answer: improper BitLocker suspension for firmware update
explanation: BitLocker suspension must be explicitly performed using 'manage-bde -protectors -disable C:' or through the Control Panel. Simply logging in and shutting down does not create the TPM measurement change window needed for the BIOS update to proceed.
fixSteps: Boot the computer and enter the 48-digit BitLocker recovery key to access Windows
Open an elevated Command Prompt and run 'manage-bde -protectors -disable C:' to properly suspend BitLocker
Reapply the BIOS update and then resume BitLocker with 'manage-bde -protectors -enable C:'
